content.text: Coding agents do not produce code only as text in a chat reply — they modify files on disk. The mechanism by which an agent translates its reasoning into a concrete filesystem change is the

: a precise, bounded instruction that removes specific text and inserts new text in its place, or that rewrites a file entirely, or that expresses changes as a standard unified diff patch. Understanding the different edit strategies, when each is appropriate, and how they fail is foundational to building reliable coding agents.

A code edit in an agentic context is a structured tool call that modifies one or more source files. Unlike a human typing in an editor, an agent cannot see the file incrementally — it reads the file contents into its context window, reasons about what change is needed, then emits a tool call that describes the transformation. The tool call is executed by the host environment (the agent framework, IDE extension, or CLI harness), which applies the change to the actual file and returns a success or error result.
The three dominant edit representations used across coding-agent systems are:
(find an exact block of text and replace it),
(write the entire new content of a file), and
line markers). Each has a distinct failure mode, context-window cost, and reliability profile.
in the classical sense is the output of comparing two versions of a file and recording only the differences. It is both the artifact produced by
. In an agentic system the LLM must generate a diff rather than just read one — the model must produce syntactically correct hunk headers, accurate context lines, and properly signed
lines without access to a diff algorithm, which makes this strategy fragile when produced manually.

The edit strategy an agent uses directly affects how often it corrupts files, how much of its context window it consumes per turn, and how reviewable its changes are to a human. A poorly chosen strategy — or a correct strategy applied incorrectly — can silently overwrite working code, introduce merge conflicts, or leave the repository in a broken state.

Because agents operate autonomously over multiple turns, an early edit error compounds: if turn 3 applies a write that silently drops a function that turn 2 had introduced, the agent may not detect the regression until several turns later, or not at all. The edit mechanism is therefore a critical reliability boundary, not a cosmetic implementation detail.
The unified diff format is also the lingua franca of code review. Every pull request, every

, every code-review comment thread is expressed in this format. An agent that produces human-readable diffs is auditable at the same granularity as a human developer's commits. An agent that produces full-file rewrites is much harder to review, especially when the file is large.

Targeted edits touch only the lines that need to change, leaving the rest of the file untouched. This eliminates whole classes of accidental regressions caused by context-window truncation or hallucinated reformatting.
A diff or a string-replace call clearly shows what changed and what did not. Full-file rewrites require a separate diff pass to understand what the agent actually modified.

Expressing a three-line change as a targeted replacement consumes far fewer output tokens than regenerating a thousand-line file. This both reduces latency and preserves context budget for subsequent turns.
A string-replace that does not find its
fails loudly rather than silently corrupting the file. This gives the agent a reliable error signal to retry or re-read.
Diffs are the native unit of version control. An agent that commits a well-formed diff produces a
that is indistinguishable from a human commit in terms of reviewability and reversibility.
Diffs can be staged, stashed, cherry-picked, and rebased using standard git tooling. A sequence of well-formed agent edits integrates cleanly into team workflows.
Targeted edits require the agent to know the exact text to replace. If the LLM has an outdated or hallucinated view of the file contents — which is common when the file changed after the last
match will fail, forcing a re-read. This can turn a two-step operation into four or six steps.
Generating syntactically correct unified diffs is cognitively harder for an LLM than regenerating the full file. Models frequently produce diffs with incorrect hunk offsets (the line numbers in
), wrong context lines, or missing trailing newlines. Each of these produces a
If the file has been modified between the last read and the edit call, any targeted edit strategy may fail or produce incorrect results.
LLMs generating unified diffs frequently miscalculate line offsets, especially after the file has been modified by an earlier edit in the same session.
When an agent rewrites an entire large file, any part of the file not in its context window is at risk of being silently dropped or hallucinated.
If the text to be replaced appears more than once in the file, the replace tool may refuse to proceed or replace the wrong occurrence.
The agent's edit loop follows a consistent read-plan-edit-verify cycle. In the
step, the agent reads the target file (or the relevant portion) into its context window. Without a current view of the file, any subsequent edit is speculative. In the
step, the LLM reasons about what change is needed and which edit strategy to use. In the
step, it emits the tool call. In the
step, it re-reads the file (or examines the diff produced by the framework) to confirm the change was applied correctly, then runs any available linter or test.
(the tool used by Claude Code and several other systems), the host finds the first and only occurrence of
in the file and replaces it with

does not appear, the tool returns an error. If it appears more than once, the tool returns an error asking for more surrounding context to disambiguate. This design forces the agent to be precise and gives it a reliable feedback signal.

utility applies them. The patch command matches context lines fuzzily by default — up to three lines of context must match — which provides some tolerance for minor mismatches. The format is standardized: file headers
, then one or more hunks each beginning with
, followed by context lines (space-prefixed), removed lines (

attrs.alt: Coding agent edit pipeline: four steps (Read Context, Plan Edit, Emit Edit, Validate) with a feedback loop for failures; three edit strategies (str_replace_editor, write_file, unified diff) shown below; unified diff anatomy illustrated at the bottom.

content.text: The diagram shows the four-step edit loop at the top: the agent reads context, plans the edit, emits the tool call, then validates. A dashed feedback arrow shows that validation failures loop back to the read-and-plan steps. The middle panel shows the three edit strategies and their primary use-case profiles. The bottom panel annotates the components of a unified diff hunk.

(targeted replacement) as the default for any in-place modification of an existing file. It is the most reliable strategy when the file is in context: the agent specifies exactly what to remove and what to put in its place, and the tool fails loudly if the match is not found rather than silently corrupting the file.
the change affects a specific function, class, block, or region and the surrounding code will not change. Provide enough context in
creating a new file, or when the file is short enough to fit entirely in the LLM's output window and the change touches most of the file. Avoid write_file for files longer than roughly 200 lines.
the agent framework natively applies patches, when the change spans multiple non-contiguous hunks, or when the diff will be reviewed as a PR before being applied.
the file is larger than the model's output token budget. The model will truncate the file, silently dropping code at the end, with no error returned.
Do not use targeted replacement without first re-reading
if another edit in the same session has already modified the file. The
Read the target file (or the relevant section) into context before any edit. Never edit from memory or from a stale read.
Identify the minimal contiguous block of text that uniquely identifies the location of the change. More context lines in
is better than fewer — it reduces the chance of an ambiguous match.
as the complete replacement text. Preserve indentation and line endings exactly. A single trailing space or mixed tab/space will cause a match failure.
Emit the tool call. On error, read the error message: "no match found" means the
is wrong — re-read the file and try again. "Multiple matches found" means the block is not unique — add more surrounding context.
After a successful edit, verify by re-reading the modified region and running the linter and any relevant tests. Do not proceed to the next edit until the current one is confirmed correct.
For unified diffs, generate the diff using Python's
rather than asking the LLM to write
line numbers manually. Pass the original and modified file content as sequences of lines.
tool pattern works in Python. This is the logic a host framework runs when the agent emits the tool call.

# Simulating the str_replace_editor tool in Python
# This is the logic the host framework runs when the agent emits the tool call.

def str_replace_editor(file_path: str, old_string: str, new_string: str) -> dict:
    """
    Replaces old_string with new_string in file_path.
    Fails loudly if old_string is absent or appears more than once.
    """
    with open(file_path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        content = f.read()

    count = content.count(old_string)
    if count == 0:
        return {"success": False, "error": "old_string not found in file"}
    if count > 1:
        return {
            "success": False,
            "error": f"old_string found {count} times; add more context to make it unique",
        }

    new_content = content.replace(old_string, new_string, 1)
    with open(file_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        f.write(new_content)
    return {"success": True}


# --- The agent would emit this as a tool call ---
# Provide enough surrounding lines to guarantee uniqueness.
old = """
def process(items):
    result = []
    return items
"""
new = """
def process(items):
    result = []
    return sorted(items)  # stable output for downstream consumers
"""

result = str_replace_editor("src/utils.py", old, new)
print(result)  # {'success': True}
Generating and Applying a Unified Diff

# Generating a unified diff programmatically and applying it via patch(1)
# No dependencies beyond stdlib + system patch command.

import difflib
import pathlib
import subprocess
import tempfile
import os


def make_unified_diff(original: str, modified: str, filename: str) -> str:
    """Produce a unified diff string between two versions of a file."""
    original_lines = original.splitlines(keepends=True)
    modified_lines = modified.splitlines(keepends=True)
    diff_lines = difflib.unified_diff(
        original_lines,
        modified_lines,
        fromfile=f"a/{filename}",
        tofile=f"b/{filename}",
        lineterm="",
    )
    return "\n".join(diff_lines)


def apply_patch(file_path: str, diff_text: str) -> None:
    """Apply a unified diff to file_path using the system patch command."""
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile("w", suffix=".patch", delete=False) as tmp:
        tmp.write(diff_text)
        patch_path = tmp.name
    try:
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["patch", "-p1", file_path, patch_path],
            capture_output=True,
            text=True,
        )
        if result.returncode != 0:
            raise RuntimeError(f"patch failed:\n{result.stderr}")
    finally:
        os.unlink(patch_path)


# --- Example ---
original = pathlib.Path("src/utils.py").read_text()

# The agent produces the modified text; difflib computes the diff.
modified = original.replace(
    "    return items",
    "    return sorted(items)  # stable output",
    1,
)

diff = make_unified_diff(original, modified, "src/utils.py")
print(diff)
# --- a/src/utils.py
# +++ b/src/utils.py
# @@ -14,5 +14,5 @@
#      result = []
# -    return items
# +    return sorted(items)  # stable output

apply_patch("src/utils.py", diff)
Consider a coding agent tasked with fixing a bug in a Python web service: the
endpoint returns results in an unpredictable order because the database query lacks an
clause. The agent is given the error report and the repository root.
The agent's turn sequence: (1) search for "users" in the API routes directory; (2) read the identified file; (3) locate the query function and confirm the missing
; (4) emit a targeted replacement; (5) re-read and run tests.

# Turn 4: agent emits str_replace_editor tool call
# file_path: api/routes/users.py

# old_string — unique, includes function signature:
old = """
def get_users(db: Session) -> list[User]:
    return db.query(User).filter(User.active == True).all()
"""

# new_string — adds ORDER BY:
new = """
def get_users(db: Session) -> list[User]:
    return (
        db.query(User)
        .filter(User.active == True)
        .order_by(User.created_at.desc())
        .all()
    )
"""
# Tool result: {'success': True}

# Turn 5: agent runs tests
# bash: pytest tests/test_users.py -q
# 3 passed in 0.41s

# Turn 6: agent reads the resulting git diff for the PR
import subprocess
diff = subprocess.run(
    ["git", "diff", "HEAD", "api/routes/users.py"],
    capture_output=True, text=True,
).stdout
print(diff)
# diff --git a/api/routes/users.py b/api/routes/users.py
# --- a/api/routes/users.py
# +++ b/api/routes/users.py
# @@ -12,4 +12,9 @@ from db import Session
#  def get_users(db: Session) -> list[User]:
# -    return db.query(User).filter(User.active == True).all()
# +    return (
# +        db.query(User)
# +        .filter(User.active == True)
# +        .order_by(User.created_at.desc())
# +        .all()
# +    )

The agent modified exactly one function across six turns: two reads, one search, one edit, one test run, one diff read. The resulting git diff is small and human-reviewable. This tight read-edit-verify loop is the standard pattern for well-scoped bug fixes.
The most common failure is emitting an edit based on a file snapshot that is no longer current. If the agent made a prior edit in the same session, or the user modified the file concurrently, the
will not match. Always re-read the file immediately before constructing

When a model regenerates a file that exceeds its output token limit, the file is silently truncated. The agent may not notice: the tool call succeeds, the file exists, but it is now missing its last hundred lines. Use targeted replacement for large files and reserve
for files that are demonstrably short or newly created.
Using a short, generic fragment as
— will fail if that string appears in multiple functions. Include the full function signature and at least two context lines before and after the changed region to guarantee uniqueness.
When a model generates a unified diff directly rather than deriving it algorithmically, it often produces incorrect
utility applies hunks with up to three lines of tolerance, but a badly wrong offset causes "Hunk FAILED" errors. The reliable fix is to generate diffs using
rather than asking the LLM to write them by hand.

Skipping the re-read and lint/test step after an edit allows silent errors to compound. A successful tool return only means the text was replaced — it does not mean the resulting code is syntactically valid or logically correct. Run the fastest available verifier (linter before full test suite) after every edit.
If more than one turn has passed since the last read, re-read the file before constructing the edit. This is especially important in multi-file sessions.
Make old_string as long as needed to be unique.
Include the function signature, any relevant decorator, and surrounding blank lines. Uniqueness is more important than brevity.
verbatim from the file content returned by
. Do not normalize indentation or collapse blank lines.
When making multiple edits to the same file in one session, apply them one at a time and re-read after each before planning the next. This prevents the
of edit N from referencing text that was modified by edit N-1.
Verify with the fastest available tool first.
After every edit, run a linter or syntax check before the full test suite. A linter that catches a syntax error in 0.1 seconds beats a 30-second test run that produces a confusing import error.
If the agent framework supports patch application, use
from the original and modified content rather than asking the LLM to produce
After a group of related edits all pass verification, commit. This creates a safe rollback point and a clean, reviewable git history.
The cost of an edit operation scales primarily with context window usage, not with the size of the file on disk. A targeted replacement that sends 20 lines of
for a 500-line file consumes roughly 5,000 output tokens and may require reading 5,000 input tokens as well — 50 times the context cost for an edit that changes three lines.

Context window efficiency is the critical bottleneck in long multi-file editing sessions. Models have a finite context window; as the session accumulates tool call results, file contents, and message history, the space available for new file reads shrinks. Agents that rely on full-file rewrites exhaust the context budget faster, leading to truncation errors or degraded reasoning quality near the end of the session.
Use offset and limit parameters in
to read only the relevant section of a large file. Reading 50 lines around the change is usually sufficient for a targeted edit.
Batch edits to the same file.
When multiple changes are needed in the same file, plan them all from a single read and apply them in sequence rather than reading the file once per edit.
Use search instead of read for discovery.
A grep-style search that returns only matching lines is far more context-efficient than reading entire files to find the relevant function.
Latency scales with token count, not file count.
An agent making ten targeted two-line edits in ten different files has a lower latency and cost profile than one making a single
str_replace is default for existing files; write used for new files
Custom syntax variant of targeted replacement; also supports whole-file mode
Runs in a sandboxed shell; applies patches via bash edit tool
Full file rewrite is the default; suitable for shorter files
IDE shows inline diff before accepting; human approves each change
Reliable algorithmic diff; use to generate diffs for patch application
Fuzzy matching on context lines; tolerates minor offset errors
Loud: patch fails on bad hunk offset
PR review workflows; fragile if LLM writes headers manually
Any workflow where agent produces modified file content
Avoid — brittle and hard to audit
The algorithmic unified diff is the gold standard when the framework supports it: the LLM produces the modified file content, the host computes the diff using

, and the diff is applied and committed. This combines high reviewability with no hallucination risk in the diff format. The downside is framework complexity — the host must implement the diff-then-apply pipeline rather than accepting a raw edit call.

An agent with write access to a codebase has the same blast radius as a developer with push access to the main branch. The key reliability mitigations are edit strategies that fail loudly rather than silently, verification steps that catch errors before they compound, and git as a safety net that allows any change to be rolled back.
The agent should always work on a feature branch, never directly on
. This is the single most important safety measure — any catastrophic edit session can be abandoned by deleting the branch.
Commit after each logical group of edits that passes verification. This limits the rollback blast radius to the current unsaved session and produces a readable commit history.
If the agent is editing code from an untrusted source, run it in a container or virtual machine. Malicious code in the repository could exploit the agent's shell access.
In production deployments, restrict the agent's file write access to the directories relevant to the task. An agent fixing a bug in
should not have write access to
Never suppress lint errors to pass tests.
to silence a real problem is masking a bug. Treat suppression-only edits as failed edits.
Require human review for security-sensitive paths.
Changes to authentication, authorization, cryptography, or dependency pinning should be reviewed by a human before merging, regardless of the agent's test-passing status.

Think of the agent as a surgeon and the codebase as the patient. A surgeon does not replace the entire body to fix one organ — they make a precise incision, modify the minimum necessary tissue, and close the wound cleanly. The targeted string-replace is the incision: specific, bounded, reversible. The full-file rewrite is more like a transplant: justified for a new or critically damaged organ, but not for a routine repair.

The pre-operative check (re-reading the file before editing) corresponds to the imaging scan before surgery: an accurate picture of the current state, not memory from a previous appointment. The post-operative validation (linter and tests) corresponds to recovery monitoring: confirming the patient is stable before closing the session.

The unified diff is the surgical report: a precise, permanent record of every incision and suture that any other surgeon can read and audit. It is not needed for the surgery to succeed, but it is essential for accountability, peer review, and learning from complications.

The three dominant edit strategies are targeted string replacement (str_replace_editor), full-file overwrite (write_file), and unified diff patch; each has a distinct failure mode and context cost — targeted replacement should be the default for modifying existing files.
Always re-read the target file immediately before constructing an edit; stale context is the primary cause of edit failures in multi-turn sessions.

str_replace_editor fails loudly when old_string is not found or is not unique — this is a feature, not a bug; it gives the agent a reliable signal to re-read and retry rather than silently corrupting the file.
write_file silently truncates files that exceed the model's output token budget; never use it for files longer than roughly 200 lines.
Generate unified diffs algorithmically using difflib.unified_diff rather than asking the LLM to produce @@ line numbers manually; LLM-generated diff headers frequently contain incorrect line offsets.
Verify after every edit by running the fastest available checker (linter or syntax validator) before proceeding to the next edit or the full test suite.

Work on a feature branch and commit at stable checkpoints; git is the safety net that makes the entire edit loop recoverable and produces a human-reviewable audit trail in the standard unified diff format.
